_note: Reference data the LLM selects from at session close. NOT training data. Descriptions written fresh; 'source' names the framework each technique comes from.

techniques.name: Box breathing
techniques.instructions: Breathe in for 4 counts, hold for 4, out for 4, hold for 4. Repeat for 2 minutes.
techniques.why: A longer, controlled exhale slows the heart rate through the vagus nerve.

techniques.name: Double inhale, long exhale
techniques.instructions: Two short inhales through the nose, then one long slow exhale through the mouth. Repeat 5 times.
techniques.why: Reinflates collapsed air sacs and offloads carbon dioxide quickly, which drops arousal within about a minute.

techniques.name: Cold water on the face
techniques.instructions: Hold cold water or a cold pack against the face around the eyes and upper cheeks for 30 seconds while holding your breath.
techniques.why: Triggers the mammalian dive reflex, which slows heart rate sharply. Fast-acting when nothing else lands.

techniques.name: Progressive muscle relaxation
techniques.instructions: Tense one muscle group hard for 5 seconds, then release. Move from feet upward through the body.
techniques.why: Tension is easier to release after deliberately increasing it. Also redirects attention into the body.

techniques.name: Shaking it out
techniques.instructions: Stand and shake out your hands, then arms, then legs, then the whole body. Two to three minutes, loosely, no form required.
techniques.why: Discharges held tension and raises physical activation when the system has gone still or shut down.

techniques.name: 5-4-3-2-1 senses
techniques.instructions: Name 5 things you can see, 4 you can hear, 3 you can touch, 2 you can smell, 1 you can taste.
techniques.why: Pulls attention out of the head and back into the immediate physical environment. Works anywhere, needs nothing.
techniques.source: Standard grounding technique (widely used in trauma-informed care)

techniques.name: One minute of intense movement
techniques.instructions: Do one minute of hard physical movement: jumping jacks, running in place, stairs. Enough to be out of breath.
techniques.why: Physical exertion shifts a stalled nervous system state. Blunt but effective when the system is flat.

techniques.name: Humming or long vocal tone
techniques.instructions: Hum a low steady note on a long exhale. Repeat for 1 to 2 minutes and feel the vibration in the chest and throat.
techniques.why: Vocal vibration stimulates the vagus nerve directly, which supports a shift out of a shut-down state.

techniques.name: Orienting to the room
techniques.instructions: Slowly turn your head and scan the room. Let your eyes land on objects and silently name them. Do not rush.
techniques.why: Signals to a hypervigilant system that the immediate environment is safe, which interrupts the loop.

techniques.name: Scheduled worry window
techniques.instructions: Pick a fixed 15 minute slot later today. When the thought returns before then, note it down and tell yourself it gets that slot, not now.
techniques.why: Containing rumination to a bounded time reduces how much of the day it occupies, without suppressing it.

techniques.name: Feet on the floor
techniques.instructions: Press both feet flat into the floor. Push down and notice the pressure, the contact, the support underneath.
techniques.why: A minimal grounding action for when a longer practice is not realistic. Physical contact anchors attention to the present.

techniques.name: Naming the feeling
techniques.instructions: Say out loud, or write down, one sentence: 'Right now I am feeling ___.' Just the name. No explanation, no fixing.
techniques.why: Putting a specific label on an emotional state measurably lowers its intensity. Naming beats suppressing.

journaling_styles.name: Fixed morning questions
journaling_styles.for: People who freeze at a blank page and want a clear frame.
journaling_styles.format: Answer the same 3 questions every morning: What is on my mind? What is one thing in my control today? What is one thing I will let go of?

journaling_styles.name: One prompt at a time
journaling_styles.for: People who get overwhelmed by a full page and stop before starting.
journaling_styles.format: One short prompt, one short answer. Two or three sentences is a complete entry. Nothing more is required.

journaling_styles.name: Draw it instead
journaling_styles.for: People who process visually or find words effortful.
journaling_styles.format: Draw the feeling. Shape, colour, scribble, whatever. No skill needed, no one else sees it.

journaling_styles.name: Unstructured brain dump
journaling_styles.for: People with a lot circling who need it out of their head first.
journaling_styles.format: Write continuously for 10 minutes without stopping or editing. Nothing has to be coherent or kept.

journaling_styles.name: Voice note journaling
journaling_styles.for: People who talk more easily than they write.
journaling_styles.format: Record a 2 minute voice note to yourself. No transcription, no rereading. The point is saying it out loud.

journaling_styles.name: Evidence log
journaling_styles.for: People stuck in an absolute belief about themselves or a situation.
journaling_styles.format: Write the belief at the top. Underneath, list only concrete evidence for and against it. Facts only, no interpretation.
